title: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome
oneLiner: Rare, life-threatening reaction to antipsychotics (or withdrawal of dopamine agonists) with hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, and altered mental state.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Severe hyperthermia (core temp >40°C) → rhabdomyolysis → acute kidney injury / multi-organ failure**  
  *Single finding:* rising serum creatinine kinase (CK) > 5000 IU/L or dark urine (myoglobin) with oliguria.
- **Autonomic instability → hypotension / arrhythmias**  
  *Single finding:* labile BP (swings >30 mmHg) or atrial fibrillation with no other cause.
- **Respiratory failure from severe rigidity / aspiration**  
  *Single finding:* oxygen saturations <92% or new tachypnoea with "lead pipe" rigidity on exam.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (>38.5°C, often >40°C) – can be the first sign
  - "Lead pipe" rigidity (not just cogwheel) – generalised, severe
  - Altered mental state: confusion, agitation, mutism, catatonia
  - Autonomic dysfunction: tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis, tachypnoea
  - Dysphagia / sialorrhea / urinary retention
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hyperthermia + rigidity + autonomic changes = triad in >90%
  - Muscle rigidity can progress to opisthotonos
  - Does **not** have clonus or hyperreflexia (distinguishes from serotonin syndrome)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Serotonin syndrome** – most common mimic; has clonus, hyperreflexia, ocular clonus, tremor; onset within 12 hours of serotonergic drug
- **Malignant hyperthermia** – history of volatile anaesthetic or suxamethonium; rapid onset in theatre; rigor + hyperthermia but no autonomic instability
- **CNS infection (meningitis / encephalitis)** – neck stiffness, photophobia, CSF pleocytosis; rigorous fever but rigidity is not "lead pipe"
- **Catatonia** – no fever, no autonomic instability; responds to lorazepam
- **Heatstroke** – no rigidity, history of exertion / environmental heat; CK usually normal
- **Anticholinergic delirium** – dry skin, dilated pupils, no rigidity; resolves with physostigmine (rarely used)
- **Splitter:** *Clonus + hyperreflexia → serotonin syndrome; lead-pipe rigidity + bradykinesia → NMS*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Core temperature (tympanic / rectal) – document >38.5°C
  - Full set of vital signs every 15 min
  - ECG – look for arrhythmias / QTc prolongation (common with antipsychotics)
  - Urine dipstick for blood (myoglobinuria) → send for urine myoglobin if positive
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, coagulation screen
  - **CK** – cornerstone; >1000 IU/L suggests rhabdomyolysis; >5000 IU/L is severe
  - Blood cultures (to exclude sepsis)
  - Drug screen (if uncertain history – cocaine / MDMA can mimic)
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head (non-contrast) – only if focal neurology / seizure / head injury to exclude structural cause
  - Consider LP if CNS infection still suspected after negative CT
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **Levenson criteria** (most used):
    - **Major:** fever, rigidity, elevated CK
    - **Minor:** tachycardia, abnormal BP, tachypnoea, altered consciousness, diaphoresis, leucocytosis, metabolic acidosis
    - Diagnosis: 3 major OR 2 major + 4 minor
- **Never-miss test:** **CK** – absence rules out NMS (but beware early sampling; repeat if low suspicion)
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - **STOP all antipsychotics** – including depot, PRN haloperidol, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, lithium (rarely implicated)
  - **Start cooling** – paracetamol 1 g IV/PO (limited central effect but low harm), cold IV fluids (500–1000 mL 0.9% saline bolus if hypotensive), ice packs, cooling blanket
  - **IV fluids** – 0.9% normal saline 200–300 mL/h initially (avoid Ringer's lactate – may worsen acidosis)
  - **Mannitol or sodium bicarbonate** – only if CK >10,000 and urine output falling; [check local policy for dose]
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Admit to HDU/ICU** if: temp >40°C, CK >10,000, autonomic instability, NEWS2 ≥6
  - **Dantrolene** – first-line for severe rigidity/hyperthermia; dose: [check local policy – typical IV 1–2.5 mg/kg bolus, repeat every 6 h up to 10 mg/kg/day]
  - **Bromocriptine** – oral dopamine agonist; start [check local policy – typical 2.5 mg TDS, titrate to 10–20 mg TDS] – avoid if hypotension
  - Monitor CK 6–12 hourly, urine output, renal function, coagulation
  - **NBM** initially – high aspiration risk from rigidity; consider NG tube for hydration/drugs
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Do **not** restart same antipsychotic for at least 2–4 weeks
  - Consider alternative class (e.g., clozapine or quetiapine) under psychiatric supervision
  - Some patients require 6–12 months of observation before re‑challenge
- **Default Orders:**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% normal saline at 150–200 mL/h (adjust for hydration status)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) – unless high bleeding risk (DIC)
  - **Diet:** NBM until swallowing safe; then low-residue if diarrhoea present
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 1–2 hours initially; then 4 hourly once stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
*(All doses – [check local policy] – never exceed recommended BNF)*
- **First-line:**
  - **Dantrolene** – IV 1–2.5 mg/kg bolus; repeat every 6 h; max 10 mg/kg/day
  - **Bromocriptine** – PO 2.5–5 mg TDS; increase to 10–20 mg TDS over 48 h
- **Alternatives:**
  - **Lorazepam** – for catatonic features / agitation 0.5–2 mg IV/PO (not first-line for NMS)
  - **Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT)** – if refractory to medical therapy (psychiatric input)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dantrolene: causes hepatic impairment; avoid in active liver disease
  - Bromocriptine: risk of hypotension, dysrhythmia; avoid in ischaemic heart disease
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Dantrolene – use with caution in hepatic impairment (reduce dose)
  - Bromocriptine – no major dose adjustment for renal/hepatic but monitor
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Continuing antipsychotics while managing NMS** – the single biggest harm. Even a single dose of haloperidol PRN can worsen hyperthermia and rhabdomyolysis.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Rhabdomyolysis → acute kidney injury (up to 30% of cases)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (atrial/ventricular)
  - Aspiration pneumonia / respiratory failure
  - Seizures (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual parkinsonism (if muscle rigidity prolonged >3 days)
  - Cognitive deficits (rare; usually reversible)
  - Recurrence if antipsychotic restarted too early (<2 weeks)
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality ~5–10% with early recognition; up to 20% if missed (older data). Recovery over 7–10 days with treatment; CK normalises in 2–3 weeks. Most patients return to baseline function.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Check CK immediately** – it’s your diagnostic and severity marker. Repeat every 6 h.
- **Monitor urine output** – aim >0.5 mL/kg/h; if falling, start aggressive fluids and consider bicarbonate or mannitol (per local policy).
- **Involve ICU early** – hyperthermia + rigidity + autonomic instability is a high-risk combination even if NEWS2 ≤5.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Mistaking for sepsis** – giving broad-spectrum antibiotics without stopping antipsychotics delays correct treatment. Always stop all antipsychotics first.
- **Using anticholinergics** (e.g., procyclidine, benztropine) – they worsen hyperthermia and do not treat NMS.
- **Restarting the same antipsychotic too quickly** – risk of recurrence; wait >2 weeks and switch class.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Fever reoccurs (>38°C)
  - Reduced urine output or dark urine
  - New confusion, severe muscle cramps, or difficulty swallowing
- **Follow-up:**
  - Psychiatric clinic within 1 week for medication review
  - Renal function (U&E + CK) within 48–72 h if CK was elevated at discharge
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / critical care outreach if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥6 (particularly if respiratory rate >25 or SpO2 <92%)
  - Core temperature >40°C despite cooling
  - CK >10,000 IU/L (or rising rapidly)
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 consecutive hours
  - GCS <12 or new focal neurology
  - Any tachyarrhythmia (atrial fibrillation, VT)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Need for central line for vasopressors (refractory hypotension)
  - Intubation required for airway protection or respiratory failure
  - Seizures not controlled with benzodiazepines

rawMarkdown:
## Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome

**One-liner:** Rare, life-threatening reaction to antipsychotics (or withdrawal of dopamine agonists) with hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, and altered mental state.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Severe hyperthermia (core temp >40°C) → rhabdomyolysis → acute kidney injury / multi-organ failure**  
  *Single finding:* rising serum creatinine kinase (CK) > 5000 IU/L or dark urine (myoglobin) with oliguria.
- **Autonomic instability → hypotension / arrhythmias**  
  *Single finding:* labile BP (swings >30 mmHg) or atrial fibrillation with no other cause.
- **Respiratory failure from severe rigidity / aspiration**  
  *Single finding:* oxygen saturations <92% or new tachypnoea with "lead pipe" rigidity on exam.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Fever (>38.5°C, often >40°C) – can be the first sign
  - "Lead pipe" rigidity (not just cogwheel) – generalised, severe
  - Altered mental state: confusion, agitation, mutism, catatonia
  - Autonomic dysfunction: tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis, tachypnoea
  - Dysphagia / sialorrhea / urinary retention
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Hyperthermia + rigidity + autonomic changes = triad in >90%
  - Muscle rigidity can progress to opisthotonos
  - Does **not** have clonus or hyperreflexia (distinguishes from serotonin syndrome)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Serotonin syndrome** – most common mimic; has clonus, hyperreflexia, ocular clonus, tremor; onset within 12 hours of serotonergic drug
- **Malignant hyperthermia** – history of volatile anaesthetic or suxamethonium; rapid onset in theatre; rigor + hyperthermia but no autonomic instability
- **CNS infection (meningitis / encephalitis)** – neck stiffness, photophobia, CSF pleocytosis; rigorous fever but rigidity is not "lead pipe"
- **Catatonia** – no fever, no autonomic instability; responds to lorazepam
- **Heatstroke** – no rigidity, history of exertion / environmental heat; CK usually normal
- **Anticholinergic delirium** – dry skin, dilated pupils, no rigidity; resolves with physostigmine (rarely used)
- **Splitter:** *Clonus + hyperreflexia → serotonin syndrome; lead-pipe rigidity + bradykinesia → NMS*

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Core temperature (tympanic / rectal) – document >38.5°C
  - Full set of vital signs every 15 min
  - ECG – look for arrhythmias / QTc prolongation (common with antipsychotics)
  - Urine dipstick for blood (myoglobinuria) → send for urine myoglobin if positive
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC, U&E, LFT, CRP, coagulation screen
  - **CK** – cornerstone; >1000 IU/L suggests rhabdomyolysis; >5000 IU/L is severe
  - Blood cultures (to exclude sepsis)
  - Drug screen (if uncertain history – cocaine / MDMA can mimic)
- **Imaging:**
  - CT head (non-contrast) – only if focal neurology / seizure / head injury to exclude structural cause
  - Consider LP if CNS infection still suspected after negative CT
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring:**
  - **Levenson criteria** (most used):
    - **Major:** fever, rigidity, elevated CK
    - **Minor:** tachycardia, abnormal BP, tachypnoea, altered consciousness, diaphoresis, leucocytosis, metabolic acidosis
    - Diagnosis: 3 major OR 2 major + 4 minor
- **Never-miss test:** **CK** – absence rules out NMS (but beware early sampling; repeat if low suspicion)

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - **STOP all antipsychotics** – including depot, PRN haloperidol, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, lithium (rarely implicated)
  - **Start cooling** – paracetamol 1 g IV/PO (limited central effect but low harm), cold IV fluids (500–1000 mL 0.9% saline bolus if hypotensive), ice packs, cooling blanket
  - **IV fluids** – 0.9% normal saline 200–300 mL/h initially (avoid Ringer's lactate – may worsen acidosis)
  - **Mannitol or sodium bicarbonate** – only if CK >10,000 and urine output falling; [check local policy for dose]
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - **Admit to HDU/ICU** if: temp >40°C, CK >10,000, autonomic instability, NEWS2 ≥6
  - **Dantrolene** – first-line for severe rigidity/hyperthermia; dose: [check local policy – typical IV 1–2.5 mg/kg bolus, repeat every 6 h up to 10 mg/kg/day]
  - **Bromocriptine** – oral dopamine agonist; start [check local policy – typical 2.5 mg TDS, titrate to 10–20 mg TDS] – avoid if hypotension
  - Monitor CK 6–12 hourly, urine output, renal function, coagulation
  - **NBM** initially – high aspiration risk from rigidity; consider NG tube for hydration/drugs
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Do **not** restart same antipsychotic for at least 2–4 weeks
  - Consider alternative class (e.g., clozapine or quetiapine) under psychiatric supervision
  - Some patients require 6–12 months of observation before re‑challenge
- **Default Orders:**
  - **IV fluids:** 0.9% normal saline at 150–200 mL/h (adjust for hydration status)
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) – unless high bleeding risk (DIC)
  - **Diet:** NBM until swallowing safe; then low-residue if diarrhoea present
  - **Observations:** NEWS2 every 1–2 hours initially; then 4 hourly once stable

### Drugs & Doses
*(All doses – [check local policy] – never exceed recommended BNF)*
- **First-line:**
  - **Dantrolene** – IV 1–2.5 mg/kg bolus; repeat every 6 h; max 10 mg/kg/day
  - **Bromocriptine** – PO 2.5–5 mg TDS; increase to 10–20 mg TDS over 48 h
- **Alternatives:**
  - **Lorazepam** – for catatonic features / agitation 0.5–2 mg IV/PO (not first-line for NMS)
  - **Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT)** – if refractory to medical therapy (psychiatric input)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Dantrolene: causes hepatic impairment; avoid in active liver disease
  - Bromocriptine: risk of hypotension, dysrhythmia; avoid in ischaemic heart disease
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Dantrolene – use with caution in hepatic impairment (reduce dose)
  - Bromocriptine – no major dose adjustment for renal/hepatic but monitor
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Continuing antipsychotics while managing NMS** – the single biggest harm. Even a single dose of haloperidol PRN can worsen hyperthermia and rhabdomyolysis.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Rhabdomyolysis → acute kidney injury (up to 30% of cases)
  - Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)
  - Cardiac arrhythmias (atrial/ventricular)
  - Aspiration pneumonia / respiratory failure
  - Seizures (rare)
- **Chronic:**
  - Residual parkinsonism (if muscle rigidity prolonged >3 days)
  - Cognitive deficits (rare; usually reversible)
  - Recurrence if antipsychotic restarted too early (<2 weeks)

### Prognosis
- Mortality ~5–10% with early recognition; up to 20% if missed (older data). Recovery over 7–10 days with treatment; CK normalises in 2–3 weeks. Most patients return to baseline function.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Check CK immediately** – it’s your diagnostic and severity marker. Repeat every 6 h.
- **Monitor urine output** – aim >0.5 mL/kg/h; if falling, start aggressive fluids and consider bicarbonate or mannitol (per local policy).
- **Involve ICU early** – hyperthermia + rigidity + autonomic instability is a high-risk combination even if NEWS2 ≤5.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Mistaking for sepsis** – giving broad-spectrum antibiotics without stopping antipsychotics delays correct treatment. Always stop all antipsychotics first.
- **Using anticholinergics** (e.g., procyclidine, benztropine) – they worsen hyperthermia and do not treat NMS.
- **Restarting the same antipsychotic too quickly** – risk of recurrence; wait >2 weeks and switch class.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Fever reoccurs (>38°C)
  - Reduced urine output or dark urine
  - New confusion, severe muscle cramps, or difficulty swallowing
- **Follow-up:**
  - Psychiatric clinic within 1 week for medication review
  - Renal function (U&E + CK) within 48–72 h if CK was elevated at discharge

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / critical care outreach if:**
  - NEWS2 ≥6 (particularly if respiratory rate >25 or SpO2 <92%)
  - Core temperature >40°C despite cooling
  - CK >10,000 IU/L (or rising rapidly)
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 consecutive hours
  - GCS <12 or new focal neurology
  - Any tachyarrhythmia (atrial fibrillation, VT)
- **Call ICU if:**
  - Need for central line for vasopressors (refractory hypotension)
  - Intubation required for airway protection or respiratory failure
  - Seizures not controlled with benzodiazepines
